text: Multitasking, as most people practice it, is a misnomer. The brain doesn't process two demanding tasks simultaneously; it switches rapidly between them, paying a toll at every transition. Cognitive psychologists call this the task-switching cost: each shift depletes a small pool of executive resources, slowing processing speed and increasing error rates. Email is a particularly efficient tax collector. Because messages arrive at unpredictable intervals, workers have habituated to checking constantly as a form of pre-emptive control — not because each check reveals something urgent, but because the uncertainty itself is harder to tolerate than the interruption. The cure, paradoxically, feels more stressful than the disease.
question: According to the passage, why do workers habitually check email even when messages are rarely urgent?

options.text: Because their managers expect near-instant responses to maintain professional standing
Because the unpredictability of email arrival makes the uncertainty harder to tolerate than the interruption
Because notifications are designed with sounds that trigger an involuntary startle reflex
Because task-switching costs are lower when workers check proactively rather than reactively
